one_line_takeaway: A longitudinal, transfer-attack study showing that newer and larger LLM versions do NOT consistently gain adversarial robustness across misclassification, jailbreak, and hallucination -- with a demonstrated safety-vs-utility trade-off where hardening against jailbreaks (e.g., GPT-3.5 v1106; a fine-tuned Qwen2-7B) coincides with degraded classification robustness.
key_finding: Model upgrades/updates do not reliably improve adversarial robustness, and can regress it. GPT-3.5 v1106 is the weakest at misclassification (highest PDR in 14/15 cases; SST-2 CTS collapses to 0.189 vs 0.874/0.742 for v0613/v0125) yet is the STRONGEST at resisting jailbreak -- exposing a misclassification-vs-jailbreak trade-off the authors tie to OpenAI's new safety systems in v1106. GPT-4 v0409 (the latest GPT-4) is consistently the worst across most misclassification datasets, and GPT-4o's newest v1120 does not defend misclassification better than predecessors (though it does improve jailbreak resistance). For Llama, larger models are NOT safer: 70B models show lower jailbreak RTS than 13B/7B, contradicting prior work; Llama-3 is overall worst. Qwen models are especially vulnerable to adversarial QUESTIONS (e.g., 12/20 Qwen-7B configs show lower RTS under adversarial questions than benign). Hallucination robustness on summarization stays near random (~0.5) across families. The Qwen2-7B fine-tuning experiment reproduces the trade-off: safety fine-tuning raised jailbreak CTS 0.312->0.812 and RTS on all three attacks, but dropped SST-2 misclassification 0.838->0.522.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first longitudinal robustness study of successive versions of LLMs, asking whether model upgrades/updates actually improve adversarial robustness. Its own contribution is NOT any new attack (the authors explicitly state 'our objective here is not to devise novel adversarial attacks... rather, we use existing methods'); it is (1) an evaluation framework that injects reused adversarial examples into the in-context-learning (ICL) query components and replays them across time-ordered versions of three model families (GPT-3.5/4/4o, Llama 1/2/3 at 7B/13B/70B, Qwen 1.5/2/2.5/3 at 7B/32B/72B); (2) a version-diffing methodology distinguishing 'upgrade' (major version change) from 'update' (minor in-place change, tracked via weekly tests of GPT-3.5/4); and (3) the empirical finding that newer/larger models do not consistently become more robust. A secondary contribution (Section 8) is a controlled Qwen2-7B fine-tuning experiment on the STAIR-SFT safety dataset showing that safety alignment can raise jailbreak robustness while degrading misclassification accuracy, offering a mechanism for the observed trade-off.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with query-only access to the target LLM, no gradients or weights. Adversarial examples are crafted on surrogate/teacher models (e.g., T5, UL2, Mistral-7B, BERT, RoBERTa, Vicuna) and transferred to the target. Three attack goals, all injected into ICL query slots (Description / Demonstration / Question, marked Clean 'C' or Adversarial 'A'): (1) misclassification -- character/word perturbations to classification prompts (from PromptBench and AdvGLUE) that flip the predicted label; (2) jailbreak -- optimization-based prompt attacks (GPTFuzz, PAIR, TAP) on the adversarial description to bypass safety guardrails on forbidden questions; (3) hallucination -- forced-choice between a correct and an adversarially constructed hallucinated answer, testing whether the model can resist being misled. Zero-shot uses 4 query variants (CC/AC/CA/AA), few-shot up to 8; jailbreak uses only clean question with clean/adversarial description; hallucination uses only zero-shot single adversarial query. The novel angle is temporal: the same transferred adversarial inputs are replayed against later model versions to see if robustness improves over time.
limitations: Authors' own limitations: they did not generate fresh adversarial examples but reused existing datasets (cost of multi-week GPT querying); no universal ICL template exists, so minor wording changes drastically shift outcomes; automated evaluation of LLM outputs is itself unsolved (they lean on a GPT-4 judge for jailbreak); and closed training data means possible train/test contamination (they argue low probability but cannot verify). Reviewer-added caveats: PDR is unstable because it is a CTS/RTS ratio -- the authors themselves note low PDR often just reflects jointly collapsed CTS and RTS, so it 'cannot be the sole metric.' Robustness is measured only through transferred attacks in an ICL replay setting, not adaptive attacks tuned per version. The 'possible explanation' rests on a single-family (Qwen2-7B) fine-tuning proxy, not the actual proprietary pipelines. Weekly 'update over time' tracking covers only misclassification, not jailbreak/hallucination.

datasets_used: GLUE tasks as clean questions: SST-2, QQP, MNLI, QNLI, RTE
AdvGLUE adversarial questions: AdvSST-2, AdvQQP, AdvMNLI, AdvQNLI, AdvRTE
PromptBench-derived adversarial descriptions (42 per question dataset for GPT, 56 for Llama; 7 attack categories)
STAIR-SFT safety-alignment dataset (~20,000 samples) for the Qwen2-7B fine-tuning experiment
quantitative_results: GPT-3.5 v1106 has the highest PDR in 14 of 15 misclassification cases (weakest at misclassification) but the highest jailbreak CTS/RTS among GPT-3.5 versions (strongest at resisting jailbreak)
GPT-4 few-shot MNLI CTS = 0.871/0.837/0.859/0.685 and RTS on QACC_few = 0.877/0.827/0.850/0.652 across v0613/v1106/v0125/v0409 (latest v0409 worst)
GPT-4o QNLI accuracy under QAC_zero = 0.889/0.881/0.830 (no improvement with upgrade)
GPT-4 v0613 jailbreak CTS = 0.537 (notable vulnerability; it is the default GPT-4 version)
Qwen-7B: adversarial questions lower RTS below benign in 12/20 configs (7B), 9/15 (32B), 8/15 (72B)
Qwen2-7B fine-tuning (origin -> fine-tuned): jailbreak GPTFuzz 0.137->0.438, PAIR 0.169->0.863, TAP 0.156->0.906, CTS 0.312->0.812
Same Qwen2-7B fine-tuning degraded zero-shot misclassification: SST-2 0.838->0.522, MNLI 0.577->0.383, QQP 0.524->0.382 (utility drop from safety tuning)
Hallucination summarization RTS stays around 0.500 (near random) across Llama and Qwen families
baselines_compared: Longitudinal model VERSIONS are the primary comparison unit: GPT-3.5 (v0613/v1106/v0125), GPT-4 (v0613/v1106/v0125/v0409), GPT-4o (v0513/v0806/v1120), Llama 7B/13B/70B (v1/v2/v2C/v3/v3I), Qwen 7B/32B/72B (v1.5C/v2I/v2.5I/v3)
Jailbreak attack methods (reused, not novel): GPTFuzz, PAIR, TAP
Surrogate/teacher models used to craft transferable adversarial examples: T5, UL2, Mistral-7B, BERT, RoBERTa, Vicuna
Underlying perturbation techniques inherited via PromptBench: TextBugger, BertAttack (among 7 attack categories)
Section 8 control: original vs STAIR-SFT-fine-tuned Qwen2-7B
Metrics: CTS (utility/rejection), RTS (robust accuracy/rejection/correct-selection), PDR (performance drop rate)
